title: Role of System-Bath Interaction in Non-Markovian Quantum Brownian Otto Cycles
abstract: We study finite-time quantum Otto cycles whose working medium is a harmonic oscillator undergoing a quantum Brownian motion described by the Caldeira-Leggett model when the oscillator is in contact with heat baths in isochoric processes. The time evolution of the Otto cycle is studied by analytically solving the exact Heisenberg-Langevin equations for the system variables and the interaction energy between the system and the bath. This enables us to investigate non-Markovian strong-coupling effects on the quantum Otto cycle. We obtain cyclic steady states and study the thermodynamic properties of the Otto cycle for various values of the parameters describing the heat baths and the coupling between the system and the bath. We compare our results with those obtained in the Markovian limit, where the time evolution is described by the Lindblad equation. We find that the change in the interaction energy during the isochoric process contributes to both work and heat, and plays a crucial role in determining thermodynamic behavior of the cycle. In particular, we find that when the Otto cycle operates as an engine, the effect of the interaction energy is to reduce the work output. We also compare our results with the power-efficiency trade-off relation recently proposed for the Markovian quantum Otto engine. We find that the power of our non-Markovian engine for a given efficiency value falls below the Markovian power-efficiency bound.
summary: This paper analyzes quantum Otto cycles operating in the non-Markovian regime by exactly treating the system-bath interaction. The authors demonstrate that this interaction significantly modifies the cycle's thermodynamics, showing that the Markovian approximation fails to capture the true behavior. Crucially, they show that the non-Markovian engine's performance falls below the Markovian power-efficiency bound.
main_problem: The study investigates the role of system-bath interaction in determining the thermodynamic properties of non-Markovian quantum Otto cycles.
main_result: The non-Markovian engine's power-efficiency data fall below the established Markovian power-efficiency bound, and neglecting the interaction energy leads to unphysical thermodynamic predictions.
method: The authors analytically solve the exact Heisenberg-Langevin equations to track the time evolution of the system and interaction energy, comparing these results against the standard Markovian Lindblad equation.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum harmonic oscillator (working medium) undergoing an Otto cycle, coupled to two heat baths (hot and cold) via a Caldeira-Leggett model interaction.
key_observables: Work output (W), heat absorbed (Q_h, Q_c), and the change in interaction energy during isochoric processes.
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($\gamma$), bath spectral density parameters ($\Omega$), and the duration of the isochoric processes ($	au_h$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the ability to solve the exact Heisenberg-Langevin equations, allowing for a direct comparison against the weak-coupling Markovian approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the four stages of the cycle, compare exact vs. Markovian time evolutions, and plot work, heat, efficiency, and power versus cycle time parameters, highlighting the impact of interaction energy.
paper_structure: The paper develops the framework by first defining the Hamiltonian and solving the exact dynamics via Heisenberg-Langevin equations. It then applies this to calculate work and heat for the Otto cycle, explicitly incorporating interaction energy corrections, and finally compares these non-Markovian results against the established Markovian bounds and theoretical limits.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes beyond the standard Markovian assumptions in quantum thermodynamics, providing quantitative insights into how memory effects (non-Markovianity) fundamentally alter the performance limits of quantum heat engines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram showing the four stages of the quantum Otto cycle

FIG. 2. The upper panel shows the time evolution of the state variables ⟨H⟩(red), ⟨L⟩(blue) and ⟨C⟩(green) of the periodic steady state for the Otto cycle which consists of the adiabatic processes with τh = τc = 3 and the isochoric ones with τhc = τch = 2 (see Eq. (31). The parameters used are ωc = 1, ωh = 15, Tc = 1, Th = 50, Ω= 20 and γ = 0.1. The solid lines are results from the exact solutions of the Heisenberg-Langevin equations and the dashed lines are from the Lindblad equation. The lower panel shows the time evolution of the average interaction energy ⟨HI(t)⟩during the same Otto cycle.
FIG. 3. Same as Fig. 2 except for the coupling strength γ = 0.7 between the system and the bath.

FIG. 5. Filled circles are the heat from the hot and cold bath, Qh, Qc, respectively as a function of τh for fixed τhc = 2.33 for the case shown in Fig. 4. eQh and g PQc are the corresponding heat when the interaction energy is not included (see Eq. (39)). ⟨∆H(ν) I ⟩(triangles) is the change in the interaction energy during the hot ν = h and cold ν = c isochoric processes (see Eq. (43)).

FIG. 4. The work output W (filled circles) as a function of τh for fixed τhc = 2.33. The parameters used are ωc = 1, ωh = 15, Tc = 1, Th = 100 and Ω= 10. Open circles are f W, which is the work when the interaction energy is not included (see Eq. (38)).
